evals.prompt: Hey, I need help drafting a cold email. I'm an AE at Lattice AI -- we sell an ML observability platform for engineering teams. My prospect is Dr. Kai Bruckner, VP of Engineering at VoltGrid GmbH, headquartered in Frankfurt, Germany. They just closed a EUR 45M Series B led by Earlybird Ventures about three weeks ago, they're hiring about 20 ML engineers according to their careers page, and Kai gave a talk at MLOps World last month on 'Taming Feature Drift in Production Models' that got a lot of buzz on LinkedIn. The company has around 280 employees and builds smart grid optimization software for European energy utilities. I want to reference his conference talk and connect it to how we help teams catch feature drift before it hits production. Can you draft the email? My name is Ava Sinclair, Senior AE at Lattice AI.
evals.expected_output: Output that demonstrates correct global router behaviour: (1) routes through the outreach skill, producing a cold email with a Five Laws compliance check section verifying all five laws before finalising, (2) detects Germany/EU jurisdiction and loads the EU outreach law overlay, explicitly flagging Germany's UWG consent requirement for cold email to individual addresses -- recommending corporate or role-based email addresses instead of firstname.lastname@ for the prospect, (3) includes the mandatory output header (TASK, ICP MATCH, CONFIGURATION, VERIFY DATA) at the top, (4) the email body leads with Dr. Bruckner's MLOps World talk on feature drift, not with Lattice AI's product, (5) the email is 150 words or fewer with one clear ask, (6) output ends with ALL OUTREACH OUTPUTS REQUIRE HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE SENDING.
expectations: Output routes through the outreach skill and contains a Five Laws compliance check section explicitly verifying all five laws (specific reference, lead with them, one ask, word count, banned language) -- wrong routing means no Five Laws check, no jurisdiction overlay, and a bad message that loses the deal

Output explicitly flags Germany's UWG consent requirement for cold email to individual email addresses and recommends using a corporate or role-based email address (e.g. engineering@voltgrid.de or a team alias) instead of a personal firstname.lastname@ address -- missing this means the rep sends a cold email that violates German law, creating legal exposure and potential fines

Output includes the mandatory output header at the top with TASK, ICP MATCH, CONFIGURATION, and VERIFY DATA fields -- missing the header breaks the structured workflow that RevOps teams rely on for tracking and QA across all agent outputs

The email body's first sentence references Dr. Bruckner's MLOps World talk on taming feature drift or his specific work -- it does not begin with 'We', 'I', 'Lattice AI', 'My company', or any sender-focused language -- violating Law 2 (lead with their problem) means the prospect deletes immediately

Output includes GDPR-relevant guidance such as documenting legitimate interest for the outreach, including an opt-out mechanism, and noting data minimisation requirements from the EU overlay -- these are not optional footnotes but jurisdiction-mandated requirements that the router must surface when EU/Germany is detected

Output ends with ALL OUTREACH OUTPUTS REQUIRE HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE SENDING -- this is the governance guardrail separating agent-drafted output from human-approved sends, and its absence means the rep may treat the draft as ready-to-send

evals.prompt: I just got off a discovery call with Priya Sharma, Head of Ops at NovaTech Solutions. They're a Series C logistics SaaS company, about 600 employees, headquartered in Singapore. Priya is based in their Mumbai office. Call went really well -- she's dealing with serious pain around pipeline visibility, said her team is 'flying blind on deal progression' and spending half their Monday mornings manually pulling reports from three different systems. She mentioned they've got budget allocated for Q2 tooling decisions and said she'd loop in their CTO Arjun Mehta for a technical eval next week. I need three things from you: (1) update her CRM record with the intel I just gathered on this call, (2) re-score this lead now that we've had first discovery contact and she's confirmed budget and next steps, and (3) draft a follow-up email summarising what we discussed and confirming the CTO meeting. My name is Carlos Reyes, AE at PipelineIQ -- we sell pipeline analytics for revenue teams.
evals.expected_output: Output that demonstrates correct multi-skill routing: the router identifies three distinct tasks and addresses all three -- (1) CRM enrichment with structured fields from the discovery call intel, (2) lead re-scoring reflecting the discovery call completion as an Engagement signal and the CTO meeting plus Q2 budget confirmation as Timing signals, and (3) a post-discovery follow-up email referencing Priya's specific language about pipeline visibility pain and confirming the CTO technical eval. Output includes the mandatory header with ICP MATCH assessment, does not fabricate any details about NovaTech beyond what was provided, and addresses Singapore jurisdiction for any outreach considerations.
expectations: Output addresses ALL THREE requested tasks (CRM enrichment, lead re-scoring, follow-up email) in a single structured response -- missing any one of the three means the rep has to come back and ask again, breaking workflow continuity and risking a dropped ball on CRM hygiene or lead prioritisation

Lead score reflects the discovery call completion as an Engagement signal AND the confirmed CTO meeting plus Q2 budget allocation as Timing signals for deal progression -- scoring without incorporating these new signals means the lead stays at its pre-call priority level, which could delay SLA-driven actions like senior AE involvement or executive sponsorship

Follow-up email references specific details from the discovery call -- Priya's pain around pipeline visibility, the 'flying blind on deal progression' language, Monday morning manual reporting, and the CTO technical eval next week -- a generic follow-up that doesn't mirror what the prospect actually said signals the rep wasn't listening, and the deal cools

CRM enrichment section structures the new call intelligence into discrete fields (pain point: pipeline visibility, budget: Q2 allocated, next step: CTO technical eval, key quote, contact added: Arjun Mehta CTO) rather than dumping raw notes -- unstructured CRM data is lost data for the next rep, the manager, or the renewal team

Output does NOT fabricate details about NovaTech Solutions that were not provided in the prompt -- no invented revenue figures, no made-up product details, no assumed tech stack -- fabricated data entering CRM destroys trust the moment the prospect or a colleague notices it

Output includes the mandatory output header with TASK description and ICP MATCH assessment -- this header is what drives whether the deal gets senior AE attention or sits in the queue, and its absence means RevOps has no structured signal to route on

evals.prompt: Hey, quick one with two parts. We're about to close a $400K enterprise deal with Meridian Corp and their legal team just sent back redlines on our MSA. They want to drop the limitation of liability cap from 2x contract value down to 1x, strike the indemnification clause entirely, and add a 90-day termination for convenience that isn't in our standard terms. My VP of Sales is asking me whether we should accept these terms and what our financial exposure looks like if we agree to the liability cap change. That's the first part. Second part -- I've got a meeting with Meridian's procurement team tomorrow morning to discuss pricing and implementation timeline, and I haven't prepped at all. Can you pull together a pre-call brief for that procurement meeting? I need to walk in knowing what to expect. My name is Jordan Nakamura, Enterprise AE.
evals.expected_output: Output that demonstrates correct scope boundary enforcement: the router (1) explicitly refuses the legal analysis portion -- MSA redline evaluation, liability cap exposure assessment, indemnification clause advice, and whether to accept the termination for convenience term -- stating clearly that legal/contract analysis is outside the scope of sales, marketing, and RevOps workflows, and recommending qualified legal counsel, (2) DOES produce the pre-call brief for the procurement meeting since meeting preparation is squarely in scope, including deal context ($400K enterprise, MSA negotiation phase) and procurement-specific preparation, and (3) does NOT provide partial legal analysis disguised as 'general business context' or 'things to consider.' Output includes the mandatory header even though part of the request was refused.
expectations: Output explicitly refuses the legal analysis portion (MSA redlines, liability cap exposure, indemnification clause advice, termination for convenience assessment) with a clear statement that contract and legal analysis is outside the scope of sales, marketing, and RevOps workflows -- giving bad legal advice on a $400K deal is catastrophic risk, and even well-intentioned analysis from a sales agent could be relied upon inappropriately

The refusal directs the user to consult qualified legal counsel -- not 'here's my unofficial take' or 'I can't give legal advice but generally speaking' or 'for reference only, these are some considerations' -- half-answers on legal questions are worse than no answer because they create false confidence

Output DOES produce a pre-call brief for the procurement meeting tomorrow since meeting preparation is an in-scope sales task -- refusing the entire request because one part is out of scope leaves the rep walking into a procurement meeting unprepared, which is a deal risk the agent could have prevented

The pre-call brief includes relevant deal context ($400K enterprise deal, MSA negotiation phase, procurement stakeholders) to help the rep prepare for the specific dynamics of a procurement conversation at this deal stage -- a context-free brief with generic procurement tips is a useless brief

Output does NOT provide even partial legal analysis disguised as 'general business context' or 'things to consider' or 'from a deal strategy perspective, these redlines might mean...' -- scope creep on legal analysis creates liability, and the boundary must be clean, not blurred with caveats

Output includes the mandatory output header (TASK, ICP MATCH, CONFIGURATION, VERIFY DATA) even on a response where part of the request was refused -- partial outputs must still maintain the workflow structure that RevOps teams depend on for consistency across all agent interactions
